top_3_failure_reasons: Speculation markets can preserve nonzero prices on famous but legally blocked names.
A fringe constitutional theory could attract attention even if it is weak.
Traders may confuse general fame with actual constitutional viability.
contradiction_forced.market_might_be_right_because: Obama remains one of the most famous Democrats in the world.
contradiction_forced.thesis_invalidation_trigger: Credible legal movement suggesting a viable path around the Twenty-Second Amendment, which is not visible now.

research_notes.primary_sources: Congress.gov Twenty-Second Amendment text; Polymarket rules page.
research_notes.historical_precedents: No modern two-term president has had a normal path back to election because the constitutional bar is explicit.
research_notes.institutional_constraints: The nomination market resolves on someone who wins and accepts the Democratic nomination for president; constitutional ineligibility is a direct structural barrier.
research_notes.timeline_recent: Obama remains publicly influential but not a viable electable nominee under the constitutional term-limit text.
research_notes.actors_and_incentives: Only speculative fame supports the contract; institutional party incentives cut the other way.
research_notes.key_uncertainties: Mostly legal-theoretical edge cases rather than political dynamics.
research_notes.counterarguments: There is no serious current pathway around the amendment's plain text.
research_notes.dominant_market_narrative: Obama is a fame-driven but constitutionally blocked longshot.
